Personal Trading · Private Capital
JaceyTrayd is the personal trading brand of John Christopher — an active trader who privately borrows capital from known individuals and shares returns with them directly.

This is not a fund, firm, or financial service. There is no public offering, no solicitation, and no open enrolment. Access is strictly by personal invitation.

About JaceyTrayd
Who we are & how we operate
My name is John Christopher. I am a trader. I trade cash and derivatives markets for a living — actively, personally, and with my own hands on every position.
JaceyTrayd is not a company. It is not a fund. It has no partners, no employees, and no regulatory standing. It is simply the name I operate under — a personal brand that belongs entirely to me.
Why You Are Here
I borrow capital from people I know personally and trust completely. In return, I share a portion of what my trading generates. That is the entire arrangement. There is no intermediary, no matching service, no pool of investors. You lend to me. I trade with it. I pay you back — with returns — as agreed.
What I Do With Your Money
Active Trading
Direct deployment in cash and derivatives markets through short-term positions.
Working Capital
Day-to-day operational needs that keep the trading business running.
Margin Requirements
Short-term margin funding for open positions and exchange obligations.
Algo & Tools
Subscriptions, data feeds, and algorithmic trading infrastructure.
Scope
Every use stays within the same trading business. Nothing goes outside of it.
How Your Returns Are Structured
Senior Creditors
Fixed monthly return on principal. Paid every month. Amount agreed at the time of joining.
Zero-Coupon Notes
Interest accrues through the tenure. Folded into a single lump-sum repayment at maturity. No monthly payouts.
Bridge Capital
Short-horizon. Terms agreed individually — amount, duration, and return discussed directly before each arrangement.
Nothing changes without a direct conversation between us.
Starting Out
My Advice
Start small. Always. I actively encourage new participants to test the waters with the least capital they are comfortable with.
Build Trust First
Only once you have seen how the arrangement works — and are genuinely satisfied — should you consider adding more.
No Pressure
There is no pitch here. No target. No urgency. The decision to stay or grow is entirely yours, at your own pace.
When Returns Are Held Back
The Situation
There are rare occasions — extraordinary market conditions — where I may need to hold back a monthly return instead of paying it out.
What It Means
The return is not lost. It stays within the arrangement and is reinvested on your behalf. It will be accounted for in full.
Why I Do This
The alternative is worse. To pay out during a tight period, I would have to bring in less core, less trusted capital from outside — which dilutes the quality of the pool and introduces risk for everyone already in it.
Bridge Capital
This is precisely why the Bridge Capital group exists — short-term, flexible participants who understand the nature of this and step in when needed, so I never have to compromise the core arrangement.
What To Know
If this ever happens, I will communicate it directly. It is not a default. It is not a fraud. It is how a small, tight, trust-based arrangement weathers extraordinary conditions.
Risk, Honestly Stated
Who I Am
A person, not an institution. There is no corporate safety net behind this.
Commitment
I have always honoured every return agreed. That track record is what I stand on.
Dependency
My ability to pay depends on trading performance and personal financial position.
Your Call
Know who you are dealing with. Make an informed decision. Independent advice is always a good idea.
Your Privacy
Visibility
Nobody else in this arrangement can see your details. Not even other participants.
What Is Stored
Your name, amount, and terms — visible only to you and to me.
This Portal
Exists purely so you can check your own position, privately and securely, at any time.
Talk to Me
Questions
Reach out to me directly. You know how to find me.
Change Requests
Use the request form on your details page. It comes straight to me.
Response
No support team. No ticket system. Just me — personally.
Legal Clarity
What This Is
A private lending arrangement between individuals who know each other personally.
What This Is Not
Not a deposit. Not a security. Not a collective investment scheme. Not regulated.
Who You Lend To
John Christopher, as a private individual — on terms agreed voluntarily between us.
Public Offer
None made. None implied. Access is strictly by personal invitation.

JaceyTrayd · Private Capital
Return
Schedule
Three rate tiers based on corpus size. Returns are monthly, paid as agreed.
Tier 1 · Low
Up to ₹5,00,000
Rate
3% flat / month
Min Corpus
₹1,00,000
Monthly (max)
₹15,000
Annual (max)
₹1,80,000
Every ₹1,00,000 earns ₹3,000/month. Simple and linear throughout this tier.
Tier 2 · Mid
₹5,00,001 – ₹9,99,999
Rate
₹15,000 + 1% on excess
Effective yield
2.01% – 2.99%
Monthly range
₹15,001 – ₹19,999
Annual range
₹1,80,012 – ₹2,39,988
Base of ₹15,000 (from the first ₹5L) plus 1% per month on every rupee above ₹5,00,000. Effective yield compresses slightly as corpus grows within this tier.
Tier 3 · High
₹10,00,000 and above
Rate
2% flat / month
Min corpus
₹10,00,000
Monthly (at ₹10L)
₹20,000
Annual (at ₹10L)
₹2,40,000
Flat 2% per month on the full corpus. Linear and predictable — every ₹1,00,000 adds ₹2,000/month and ₹24,000/year.

Public Offer

A Note on Equity Holdings
What Happened
On a small number of occasions, participants asked me to purchase specific stocks on their behalf. I did so — out of goodwill, within an existing relationship, and with no intention of making it a service. The shares were bought through my own demat account and held there, pending an off-market transfer to the participant.
Where It Stands
Some of those holdings have not yet been transferred. They remain in my demat, ring-fenced in my own records as belonging to the participant who requested them. The delay is mine — I became aware that informal custodianship of this kind, even between trusted individuals, sits in regulatory grey territory I am not comfortable occupying.
What I Am Doing
I have stopped accepting new requests of this kind entirely. The outstanding holdings will be resolved — transferred or settled — as I work through the process correctly. No participant is at risk of losing what they asked me to hold.
Why I Am Saying This
Because this portal is built on honesty. If you are a participant and your name is attached to holdings in my demat, you deserve to know that I am aware of the situation, I take it seriously, and I am not waiting for it to become a problem before I act.
Knowledge Hub
Finance fundamentals · Where JaceyTrayd fits
Before you can understand what JaceyTrayd is, it helps to understand what it is not — and why the distinction matters.
01 · The Markets
Capital, Financial & Money Markets
Capital Markets
What they are
Markets where long-term instruments are traded — stocks, bonds, debentures. Think NSE, BSE, bond markets. The horizon is typically over one year.
Who uses them
Companies raising funds, governments issuing bonds, institutional investors, retail investors, and traders.
Example
Buying shares of Reliance on NSE, or subscribing to a government bond issue.
Money Markets
What they are
Markets for short-term borrowing and lending — instruments with maturities under one year. Treasury bills, commercial paper, certificates of deposit.
Who uses them
Banks, corporations, and governments managing short-term liquidity needs. Extremely low risk, extremely low return.
Example
An RBI T-bill, an overnight call money market transaction, or a liquid fund.
Financial Markets — The Full Picture
"Financial markets" is the catch-all term for any organised system where financial instruments are bought, sold, or exchanged. Capital markets and money markets are two components — but there are six distinct categories in total.
The six types of financial market
Money Markets
Short-term borrowing & lending (<1yr). T-bills, commercial paper, call money. Low risk, low return.
Capital Markets
Long-term instruments (>1yr). Stocks, bonds, debentures. NSE, BSE, bond markets.
Forex Markets
Currency exchange. USD/INR, EUR/USD etc. The world's largest market by daily volume.
Derivatives Markets
Contracts that derive value from an underlying asset. Futures, options, swaps. Used for hedging and speculation.
Commodity Markets
Physical goods traded as financial instruments. Gold, crude oil, agricultural produce. MCX in India.
Crypto Markets
Digital assets — Bitcoin, Ethereum etc. Largely unregulated globally. Highly volatile. 24/7 markets.
02 · Instruments
Equity vs Debt
Equity — you own a piece
What it is
You give money and receive ownership — a share in the company. Your return depends entirely on how the company performs. No guaranteed return. High upside, high downside.
Returns
Dividends (if declared) and capital appreciation. You can also lose your entire investment.
Example
Buying 100 shares of Infosys. You are now a tiny part-owner of Infosys.
Debt — you are the lender
What it is
You give money and receive a promise — a fixed return over a fixed or open-ended period, with your principal returned at the end. You do not own anything. You are a creditor.
Returns
Interest — fixed and agreed upfront. Predictable. Lower upside than equity, but more certainty.
Example
A fixed deposit with a bank, or buying a government bond. You lend money, they pay you interest, they return your money.
The key difference
Equity investor
Owns a stake · Profits vary · Bears the company's risk
Debt investor (creditor)
Lends money · Gets fixed return · Repaid before equity holders
03 · Pooled Vehicles
Mutual Funds vs Hedge Funds
Mutual Fund — the public pool
Structure
Money from many investors pooled together and managed by a professional fund manager. Regulated by SEBI. Open to the general public.
Strategy
Typically conservative — index funds, equity funds, debt funds. Strict limits on what they can do.
Disclosure
Daily NAV published. Full portfolio disclosure required quarterly. Highly transparent.
Hedge Fund — the private pool
Structure
Private pooled fund for wealthy or institutional investors. Lightly regulated, high minimum investment. Not open to the public.
Strategy
Can do almost anything — long/short, leverage, derivatives, distressed assets. Aim is absolute returns regardless of market direction.
Fee structure
Typically "2 and 20" — 2% management fee plus 20% of profits. Expensive for what you get unless performance is exceptional.
04 · Other Vehicles
Portfolio Management · PMS · AIF
PMS — Portfolio Management Service
A SEBI-regulated service where a professional manager invests directly in stocks on your behalf — not pooled like a mutual fund, but your own portfolio. Minimum ₹50 lakhs. Manager has discretion over individual stock picks. Returns and portfolio are yours alone.
AIF — Alternative Investment Fund
A SEBI-registered pooled vehicle for sophisticated investors. Minimum ₹1 crore. Three categories: venture capital / private equity (Cat I), hedge-style funds (Cat II), and leveraged / complex strategy funds (Cat III). Not for the general public.
Fixed Deposit & RD
The most familiar debt instrument for most Indians. You lend money to a bank for a fixed period at a fixed rate. Capital is protected (up to ₹5L per bank under DICGC insurance). Returns are modest — typically 6–8% per annum. No risk, no upside.
Chit Fund
A rotating savings and credit scheme popular in India. A fixed group of members each contribute a fixed amount every month. Each month, the pooled sum is auctioned or allocated to one member — who receives it as either a lump-sum payout or a loan, depending on the structure. Over the full cycle, every member gets one turn. The organiser (foreman) charges a small commission. Chit funds are regulated by the Chit Funds Act, 1982 and governed at the state level. They serve a dual function — savings vehicle and access to credit — within the same circle of participants.
05 · Market Infrastructure
The plumbing behind financial markets
Broker
What they do
An intermediary who executes buy and sell orders on your behalf on a stock exchange. You tell the broker what you want. The broker routes the order to the exchange. They do not hold your securities and do not own what you buy.
Regulated by
SEBI. Must be a registered member of a stock exchange — NSE or BSE. Examples: Zerodha, ICICI Direct, HDFC Securities.
Depository Participant (DP)
What they do
A DP is an agent of a central depository — the entity that actually holds your securities in electronic form. When you buy shares, they don't arrive as paper certificates. They sit in your demat account, which the DP maintains on behalf of the depository.
Regulated by
SEBI, via the two central depositories in India — NSDL and CDSL. Your broker and your DP are often the same institution, but they are legally distinct roles.
Central Depository
What they do
The institution that holds the master record of who owns what. In India: NSDL (National Securities Depository Limited) and CDSL (Central Depository Services Limited). When securities change hands, the depository updates the register. No physical movement of anything — it is all entries in a ledger.
Analogy
Think of the depository as the land registry — it holds the official title. The DP is the local registrar's office where you file paperwork. The broker is the agent who helped you buy the property.
Clearing House
What they do
When a trade is matched on an exchange, someone has to guarantee that both sides actually deliver — the buyer pays, the seller transfers the securities. The clearing house steps in between buyer and seller, becoming the counterparty to both, and settles the transaction. If one side defaults, the clearing house absorbs it.
In India
NSE Clearing Limited (for NSE trades) and BSE's clearing arm handle settlement. Trades in India settle on a T+1 basis — the transfer of securities and cash happens the next business day.
How it fits together
You instruct your Broker → order goes to the Exchange
→ Clearing House guarantees & settles the trade
→ Central Depository updates the ownership record
→ your DP reflects the change in your demat account
06 · The Position
How JaceyTrayd is none of these
Not a broker
John does not execute trades on your behalf on any exchange. He is not intermediating between you and a market. He trades his own book — with his own capital and yours combined. You are not a client of a brokerage. You are a lender to a trader.
Not a depository participant or clearing house
There are no securities held in your name here. No demat account. No settlement infrastructure. The arrangement is purely monetary — you lend capital, John deploys it, returns flow back to you. Nothing is custodied, cleared, or settled through any regulated depository mechanism.
Not a fund of any kind
There is no pooling of capital. No units. No NAV. No professional fund manager in the regulatory sense. No SEBI registration. Each arrangement is a direct, bilateral relationship between you and John Christopher — one person lends, one person borrows.
Not a deposit
A bank deposit is insured, regulated, and backed by an institution. JaceyTrayd is none of those things. Your capital is not ring-fenced or insured. The assurance is John's personal track record and the direct nature of the relationship — nothing else.
Not a collective investment scheme
A CIS pools contributions from multiple people, manages them collectively, and pays out proportional returns. JaceyTrayd does not pool. Your money is not mixed with another participant's money in a single managed pot. Each person's arrangement stands independently.
Not a P2P lending platform
To understand why, it helps to know where P2P sits in the larger map. Within capital markets, the debt (credit) side has a segment called alternative finance — lending that happens outside traditional banks. P2P lending is a category within alternative finance: a technology platform regulated by the RBI that matches individual lenders with individual borrowers they have never met, using credit scoring, underwriting algorithms, and formal legal contracts.
Where P2P sits
Financial Markets
↳ Capital Markets — Credit/Debt side
↳ Alternative Finance
↳ P2P Lending (RBI-regulated platforms)
JaceyTrayd has none of these characteristics. There is no platform. There is no matching of strangers. John is not an intermediary — he is the borrower. You are not being connected to someone else's loan book. You lend directly to one person you know personally, on terms you agree together. No algorithm, no credit score, no RBI oversight. What governs this is relationship and track record — not technology or regulation.
Not a chit fund
A chit fund pools contributions from a rotating group and uses them to provide credit or payouts to members in turn — it is simultaneously a savings scheme and a lending mechanism within the same circle. JaceyTrayd does none of this. There is no rotating pool. No member receives a lump sum that others have contributed to. No one is accessing credit through this arrangement. You lend capital to John. John trades with it. John pays you a return. That is the complete picture — there is no rotation, no auction, no group obligation.
So what is it, precisely?
A private lending arrangement between individuals who know each other. You are the lender. John is the borrower. The terms — amount, return, duration — are agreed between you directly. The arrangement is governed by mutual trust, not regulation.
In legal terms, the closest analogy is an informal private loan. In structural terms, it resembles a zero-coupon note or a senior creditor relationship — depending on the arrangement type chosen. In human terms, it is a handshake backed by a track record.
07 · Risk Literacy
What risk actually means here
Counterparty risk
The risk that the person you lent to cannot or does not repay. In all structured products — bonds, FDs, mutual funds — there are legal mechanisms and institutions standing behind the obligation. Here, the counterparty is one individual. That is the primary risk you are accepting.
Liquidity risk
You cannot sell or transfer your position. If you need your capital back ahead of schedule, it depends on John's position at that time — not on a market. This is illiquid capital for the duration of the arrangement.
Concentration risk
All returns depend on the performance and integrity of one person. Diversification — the usual risk mitigation tool — does not apply here. That is why trust, relationship, and track record are the only real due diligence available.
This page is for informational purposes only. Nothing here constitutes financial advice. JaceyTrayd does not solicit investment from the public. If you are here, you were personally referred or invited.

Our History
JaceyTrayd · Est. 30 October 2015
Nearly a decade of private capital management — built on discipline, trust, and a small circle of people who believed early.
—
AUM
—
Participants
—
Track Record
01 · Timeline
Inception & Legacy
Oct 30, 2015
Established
JaceyTrayd was founded by John Christopher as a private trading operation — personal capital, personal discipline, no external obligation.
January 2016
First Participants
Three initial investors came on board — the first to extend trust and capital to the arrangement. They remain the foundation on which everything since has been built.
2016 – Present
Growth
The arrangement grew quietly — word of mouth, personal referrals, no marketing. By the time the circle had grown to its current size — measured in tens of participants and tens of lakhs under management — it had done so entirely through demonstrated performance and personal trust.
2024
Deliberate Slowdown
New intake was intentionally reduced. No new arrangements have been taken on since. The focus shifted entirely to honouring existing commitments — cleanly, on agreed terms, without distraction.
April 2027
Planned Wind-Down
Most service offerings to external participants are planned to be concluded. Existing arrangements will be honoured in full through their agreed terms. The circle closes as it opened — quietly, and on John's own timeline.
02 · Purpose
Mission, Vision & Objective
Mission
Disciplined and prudent capital management — deploying modern research tools and systematic analysis to deliver returns that justify the trust placed in this arrangement.
Vision
To be the kind of investment partner people point to as proof that consistent long-term growth and capital preservation are not mutually exclusive — earned through a track record, not a pitch deck.
Objective
To outperform inflation and mutual fund returns through direct deployment in the capital markets — with measured caution, not reckless aggression. The goal is not to beat the market at all costs. It is to protect and grow your capital in a way that makes sense.
03 · Method
Philosophy & Approach
Investment Philosophy
Research-driven. Fundamentals first. Long-term value over short-term noise. Risk is managed, not avoided — because avoiding all risk is its own form of failure. Every position is sized and timed with discipline, not impulse.
Technology & AI
Advanced data analysis, predictive models, and AI-assisted tools are integrated into the investment process — not as a replacement for judgement, but as a sharpener of it. This portal itself is an example of that philosophy: technology in service of a human arrangement.
Investment Process
Thorough research and due diligence before any position is taken. A diversified approach across instruments and timeframes. Active monitoring, with adjustments made based on market conditions rather than emotion or calendar pressure.
04 · Who This Is For
Participant Criteria
Age & Suitability
Participation has historically been considered appropriate for individuals aged 20 to 50. The reasoning is deliberate: capital markets carry inherent risk, and retirement savings should not be exposed to them through an informal arrangement. This is discretionary capital — money you can afford to have working, not money you cannot afford to lose entirely.
A note on legacy
JaceyTrayd has never advertised. Every person who has been part of this arrangement came through a personal connection. That is not a limitation — it is the design. The circle stays small so the trust stays real.
Established 30 October 2015. First participants onboarded January 2016. Wind-down of most offerings planned for April 2027. Existing arrangements honoured in full.
Arrangement Types
I
Zero-Coupon Notes
Highest Trust
Your capital is placed with John Christopher and grows quietly over time. There are no monthly payouts — interest accrues and is consolidated into a single repayment at maturity. This arrangement requires the most patience and the deepest trust, as there is no regular touchpoint to reassure you along the way.
Return: Lump sum at maturity
Horizon: Long term
II
Senior Creditors
Ongoing
A steady, ongoing arrangement where your corpus earns a fixed monthly return — paid directly to you each month. The relationship is open-ended, with principal repayable on mutual agreement. This suits those who want regular income and are comfortable with the informal nature of the arrangement.
Return: Monthly
Horizon: Open-ended
III
Bridge Capital
Short Term
A flexible, short-horizon arrangement for need-based capital deployment. Terms are agreed directly — amount, duration, and return. There is no long-term commitment on either side. Bridge Capital is also where new relationships begin, allowing both parties to build confidence before deeper arrangements are considered.
Return: As agreed
Horizon: Short term
IV
Introduced Capital
Via Administrator
You do not have a direct relationship with John Christopher — but someone who does has vouched for you. Your capital is placed with JaceyTrayd, and your returns are managed by that person, your Returns Administrator, who is an existing Senior Creditor or Zero-Coupon Note member. Your trust is in them. Their trust is in John. The chain is short, and the capital is safe.
Return: Via administrator
Entry: By introduction only
All arrangements are strictly private and by personal invitation only.
Which arrangement suits me?
Edit Member —

Private Lending Agreement — Draft
